blocks:
Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُزَّمِّلُ (١)
O you wrapped up in your mantle! (1)
قُمِ ٱلَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٢)
Stand vigil through the night, except for a little [of it], (2)
نِّصْفَهُۥٓ أَوِ ٱنقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلًا (٣)
a half, or reduce a little from that (3)
أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ وَرَتِّلِ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَرْتِيلًا (٤)
or add to it, and recite the Quran in a measured tone. (4)
إِنَّا سَنُلْقِى عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا (٥)
Indeed soon We shall cast on you a weighty discourse. (5)

Content of Surah "Al-Muzzammil"

The tone of the verses in this Surah clearly indicates that it aligns with the "Meccan" Surahs; thus, the possibility of it being revealed in "Medina," as some have suggested, seems unlikely. However, the difference in tone between the beginning and the end of the Surah suggests a considerable time gap between the revelation of these verses, which some commentators have recorded as "eight months," some "one year," and some even "ten years." [^1] Many verses of this Surah show that they were revealed when the Prophet ص had begun his public invitation, and the opponents had risen in opposition and denial against him, and the Prophet ص was commanded to deal with them with forbearance at this stage.

Therefore, the possibility that the entire Surah was revealed at the very beginning of the Prophet's ص mission seems very unlikely. It is possible that its initial verses were so, but certainly not all its verses, because its expressions indicate the expansion of Islam—at least at the level of Mecca—and the rise of opponents in defiance and struggle, and we know that such a situation did not exist in the first three years of the mission.

The occasions of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) mentioned in narrations for the Surah or parts of its verses also vary.

[^1]: Refer to Tafsir "Al-Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 276, and "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 377.

In some narrations, it is stated that when the Prophet ص received the first divine message and revelation, he came to Khadija in a state of awe (feeling physical discomfort and seeking rest) and said: "Wrap me in a garment." It was then that Gabriel descended and brought "يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ" for the Prophet ص. [^1] Meanwhile, other narrations state that this event occurred when the Prophet ص had made his invitation public, and the Quraysh gathered in "Dar al-Nadwa" to deliberate on the Prophet's ص affair and choose a name or slogan to counter him. Some said he is a "soothsayer" (Kahin), but a group opposed this suggestion. Others said he is "possessed" (Majnun), but again a group opposed. Some preferred the title "sorcerer" (Sahir), but that too was not accepted.

Finally, they said, whatever he is, "he causes separation between friends" (therefore, he is a sorcerer). Then the polytheists dispersed.

This news reached the Prophet ص; he wrapped himself in his garments (and went to rest). At this moment, Gabriel came and recited "يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ" and "يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ" to him. [^2] The conclusion is that, as we previously noted, this Surah was apparently revealed in Mecca, and certainly a part of it was after the public emergence of Islam and its relative influence in Mecca, although it is possible that verses from the beginning of the Surah were revealed at the start of the Prophetic mission.

[^1]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 28, p. 101, and "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 446. [^2]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 6, p. 276.

In any case, the content of the Surah can be summarized into five sections:

The first section: The verses at the beginning of the Surah which invite the Prophet to nightly standing for worship and recitation of the Quran, preparing him for the acceptance of a heavy responsibility.

The second section: Invites him to patience, endurance, steadfastness, and forbearance with the opponents at this specific juncture.

The third section: Discusses the Resurrection, the sending of Moses son of Imran to Pharaoh, his rebellion, and his subsequent painful punishment.

The fourth section: Eases the strict instructions given at the beginning of the Surah regarding the nightly standing, due to the hardships faced by the Muslims.

The fifth section: Which is the final part of the Surah, once again invites to the recitation of the Quran, establishing prayer, giving Zakat, spending in the way of Allah, and seeking forgiveness.

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam ص, it is stated: "Whoever recites Surah Al-Muzzammil, hardship will be removed from him in this world and the Hereafter." [^1]

And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah Al-Muzzammil in the second Isha prayer (meaning the Isha prayer itself, as sometimes Maghrib is called the first Isha) or at the end of the night, the night and the day, as well as this Surah itself, will be witnesses for him on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah will grant him a pure life and a pure death!" [^1]

Certainly, these great virtues are attained if the content of the Surah—regarding nightly standing, recitation of the Quran, patience, steadfastness, sacrifice, and spending—is put into practice, not merely a recitation devoid of action.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 375.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ (1) قُمِ اللَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (2) نِصْفَهُ أَوِ انْقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلًا (3) أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا (4) إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا (5)

Commentary

O You Wrapped in Garments, Arise!

As is evident from the tone of the beginning of this Surah, it is a heavenly call to the Prophet ص for steadfastness and preparation to accept a great and heavy duty, which is impossible to fulfill without prior self-building. It says:

"O you wrapped in garments!" (يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ) [^1] "Stand [in prayer] the night, except for a little" (قُمِ اللَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا). "Half of it—or subtract from it a little" (نِصْفَهُ أَوِ انْقُصْ مِنْهُ قَلِيلًا). "Or add to it" (أَوْ زِدْ عَلَيْهِ). "And recite the Quran with measured pace and reflection" (وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا).

It is interesting that the addressee in these verses is the Prophet ص, but not as "O Messenger" or "O Prophet," but as "O you wrapped in garments," indicating that it is not the time for "wrapping oneself in garments" and sitting in a corner of seclusion, but rather the time for rising, self-building, and preparation for fulfilling a magnificent mission.

[^1]: "Muzzammil" was originally "Mutazammil," from the root "Tazammul," meaning to wrap a cloth around oneself. "Zamil," which means a companion (one who rides with a person on a mount) and later came to mean "friend," is because of the close connection and bond.

The choice of the night for this task is because, firstly, the eyes and ears of the enemies are asleep, and secondly, the affairs of daily life are suspended; for this reason, a person is more prepared for contemplation, reflection, and self-discipline.

Similarly, the choice of the "Quran" as the primary text of this program is because it contains all the necessary lessons in this regard and is the best means for strengthening faith, steadfastness, piety, and the nurturing of souls.

The expression "Tartil," which originally means "organization" and "rhythmic arrangement," here means reciting the verses of the Quran with deliberation and necessary order, correct pronunciation of letters, clarification of words, and careful reflection on the concepts of the verses and their implications.

It is obvious that such a recitation of the Quran can rapidly grant a person spiritual growth, moral courage, and piety. If some commentators have interpreted it as performing prayer, it is because "recitation of the Quran" is one of the major parts of prayer.

The expression "Stand" (Qum) in the phrase "Stand the night" means rising as opposed to sleeping, not just standing on one's feet.

As for the various expressions regarding the duration of the "nightly vigil" in these verses, they are actually to express "choice" (Takhyir), giving the Prophet ص the choice to stay awake for half the night, or a bit less, or a bit more, and recite the Quran. In the first stage, it mentions "the whole night except a little," then eases it to half the night, and then less than half.

Some have said the intention is a choice between "two-thirds," "half," and "one-third" of the night, based on the verse that appears at the end of this Surah, which says: "Indeed, your Lord knows that you stand almost two-thirds of the night, and half of it, and a third of it."

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this last verse of the Surah that the Prophet ص was not alone in this nightly standing; rather, a group of believers were also accompanying the Prophet ص in this program of self-building, education, and preparation, having accepted him as a role model and exemplar on this path.

Some commentators have said that the meaning of the phrase "Stand the night except for a little" is to stand on all nights except for some nights; thus, the exception is not in the parts of the night but in the individual nights. However, this interpretation does not seem correct given the singular form of "Layl" (night) and the mention of "half" and "less than that."

Then, it states the "ultimate goal" of this difficult and important command: "Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word" (إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا). Although commentators each have an explanation for "heavy word" (Qawlan Thaqila) focusing on one dimension of the issue, it seems that the heaviness of this "word"—which undoubtedly refers to the Glorious Quran—is from various aspects and dimensions:

Heavy in terms of content and the meaning of the verses! Heavy in terms of its endurance by hearts and souls, to the extent that the Quran itself says: "If We had sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled and coming apart from fear of Allah" (Al-Hashr: 21). Heavy in terms of promises, warnings, and the declaration of responsibilities. Heavy in terms of propagation and the difficulties of the path of invitation. Heavy in the scales of deeds and in the arena of Resurrection. And finally, heavy in terms of planning and its complete implementation.

Yes, although reciting the Quran is easy, beautiful, and pleasant, realizing its contents is proportionately heavy and difficult, especially at the beginning of the Prophet's ص mission and his rise in Mecca, where the environment was covered by dark clouds of ignorance, idolatry, and superstition, and prejudiced, stubborn, and ruthless enemies had joined hands against him. Yet, the Noble Prophet ص and his few companions, by seeking help from the education of the Glorious Quran, and seeking assistance through the night prayer and utilizing proximity to the Pure Essence of the Lord, were able to overcome all these difficulties and carry the burden of this "heavy word" on their shoulders to the intended destination!

Key Points

1. Nightly Standing for Quranic Recitation and Supplication

We said that although the addressee in these verses is the person of the Prophet ص, the end of the Surah shows that the believers were also accompanying the Prophet ص in this program. Now the question is: was this standing and nightly vigil mandatory for everyone at the beginning of the Prophet's ص mission or not?

Some commentators believe this was mandatory, and later the last verse of the Surah abrogated this ruling, with the interval being about one year. Some even believe that this ruling was before the legislation of the five daily prayers, and after the five prayers were legislated, this ruling was abrogated.

However, as the late "Tabarsi" also noted in "Majma' al-Bayan," nothing is seen in the apparent verses of this Surah that serves as evidence for "abrogation." It is better to say that this standing and worship was a recommended (Mustahabb) and emphasized Sunnah, and never had an obligatory (Wajib) aspect, except in the case of the person of the Noble Prophet ص, for whom, according to some other verses of the Quran, the night prayer was mandatory. There is no obstacle to this matter being mandatory for the Prophet ص and recommended for the Muslims. Furthermore, what is mentioned in the above verses is not limited to the "Night Prayer" (Salat al-Layl), because the night prayer does not occupy half the night, or two-thirds, or even one-third; what is proposed in the verse is standing for the "Tartil" of the Quran.

Therefore, at the beginning of the work, this ruling was in the form of an emphasized recommendation, and later it was eased. Given that at the start of any task, especially at the beginning of a great revolution, more power and energy are always required, it is not surprising that such an extraordinary command was given to the Prophet ص and the Muslims to stay awake for a large part of the night, to become familiar with the content of this new program and its revolutionary teachings, and in addition to awareness, to build themselves spiritually for its implementation.

2. The Meaning of Tartil

What is emphasized in the above verses is not the issue of reciting the Quran, but the issue of "Tartil." In the interpretation of "Tartil," narrations have been quoted from the Infallibles عليه السلام, each pointing to one of its vast dimensions.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Tartil means to express it clearly; do not recite it rapidly like poetry, nor scatter it like grains of sand. Rather, strike the hardened hearts with it and awaken them. Let not the goal of any of you be merely to reach the end of the Surah (the important thing is to understand the content of the verses)." [^1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of "Tartil," we read: "When you pass by a verse in which there is a mention of Paradise, ask Allah for Paradise (and prepare yourself for it), and when you pass by a verse in which there is a mention of the Fire, seek refuge in Allah from the Fire (and keep yourself away from it)." [^2]

And in another narration from the same Imam عليه السلام, it is stated regarding "Tartil": "It is that you pause during it and beautify your voice with it." [^3]

Also, in another Hadith from the same Imam, it is quoted: "The Quran should not be read hurriedly, but it should be recited with Tartil. Whenever you pass by a verse in which there is a mention of the Fire, you stop at it and seek refuge in Allah from the Fire." [^1]

Finally, it is narrated regarding the Prophet's ص state that he would separate the verses from one another and draw out his voice. [^2] These narrations and others with the same meaning quoted in "Usul al-Kafi," "Nur al-Thaqalayn," "Al-Durr al-Manthur," and other books of Hadith and Tafsir, all testify to the reality that one should not recite the verses of the Quran as words devoid of content and message. Rather, one must pay attention to all matters that deepen its impact on the reader and listener, and not forget that this is a divine message, and the goal is to realize its content.

But unfortunately, today many Muslims have distanced themselves from this reality and have sufficed with the Quran only as words. Their only concern is finishing the Surah and finishing the Quran, without knowing why these verses were revealed and what message they convey. It is true that the words of the Quran are also respected and reciting them has virtue, but it must not be forgotten that these words and recitation are a prelude to the expression of the content.

3. The Virtue of the Night Prayer

These verses once again remind us of the importance of the nightly vigil, the night prayer, and the recitation of the Quran at a time when the heedless are asleep. As we have previously mentioned, worship at night, especially at dawn and near daybreak, has an extraordinary effect on the clarity of the soul, the refinement of the self, the spiritual training of man, the purity of the heart, the awakening of the soul, the strengthening of faith and will, and the consolidation of the foundations of piety in the heart and soul of man, such that even with one trial, a person clearly feels its effects within himself.

For this reason, in addition to the verses of the Quran, great emphasis has been placed on it in Islamic narrations as well. Among them, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Three things are from the special graces of Allah the Almighty: Tahajjud at night (the night prayer), giving Iftar to those fasting, and meeting with Muslim brothers." [^1]

In another Hadith from the same Imam, we read in the interpretation of the verse "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds": "The night prayer does away with the sins of the day." [^2]

Under verse 79 of Surah Al-Isra, we also had a detailed discussion in this regard and quoted ten interesting narrations about the importance of the night prayer there. [^3]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 378—this Hadith also appears in Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, chapter "Tartil al-Quran bi al-Sawt al-Hasan," and other books with slight variations. [^2] & [^3]: Same source. [^1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 447. [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," under the verses discussed. [^1] & [^2]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 87, p. 143. [^3]: Volume 12, page 227.

Ayahs

إِنَّ نَاشِئَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ هِىَ أَشَدُّ وَطْـًٔا وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلًا (٦)
Indeed the watch of the night is firmer in tread and more upright in respect to speech, (6)
إِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ سَبْحًا طَوِيلًا (٧)
for indeed during the day you have drawn-out engagements. (7)
وَٱذْكُرِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ وَتَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلًا (٨)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord and dedicate yourself to Him with total dedication. (8)
رَّبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَٱتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلًا (٩)
Lord of the east and the west, there is no god except Him; so take Him for your trustee, (9)
وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْرًا جَمِيلًا (١٠)
and be patient over what they say, and distance yourself from them in a graceful manner. (10)

Commentary

The Impact of Supplication in the Heart of the Night

These verses continue the discussion regarding nocturnal worship and spiritual training in the light of reciting the Quran in the heart of the night. In reality, they serve as an explanation of the rationale for what was mentioned in the previous verses. It states: This command for worship and nocturnal recitation is because the program of (worship and education) at night is more stable and enduring (إِنَّ ناشِئَةَ اللَّيْلِ هِيَ أَشَدُّ وَطْئاً وَ أَقْوَمُ قِيلًا) ^1. "Nashia" (ناشئة) is derived from the root "nasha'a" (نشأ), meaning "event" or "occurrence." Regarding what is meant by it here, three interpretations have been mentioned:

First, it refers to the hours of the night that occur one after another, or specifically the final hours of the night and the dawn.

Second, it refers to the program of rising for prayer, worship, and the recitation of the Quran, as narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who said: “It is rising at the end of the night for the Night Prayer.” [^2] In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, it is stated: “It is his rising from his bed desiring nothing but Allah.” [^3]

Third, it refers to those "spiritual and mystical states" and the "celestial vigor and attraction" that arise in the heart and soul of a human during these specific hours of the night, whose effects are deeper in the human spirit and more lasting.

Of course, this interpretation and the one before it are inextricably linked, and it is appropriate for both to be included in the meaning of the verse.

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 378. [^3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 448, Hadith 16.

"Wat'an" (وطئا) originally means to tread or step, and also to agree or harmonize. The expression "ashaddu wat'an" (أشد وطئا) either refers to the hardship and effort involved in nocturnal rising and worship, or it refers to the firm and established impressions that are formed in the human soul and spirit in the light of these acts of worship (the second meaning is more appropriate).

There is also the possibility that it means the greater harmony that occurs in these moments between the human heart, eyes, and ears, and the mobilization of all of them in the path of worship.

"Aqwam" (أقوم) is derived from the root "qiyam," meaning more stable and straight, and "qila" (قيل) means "speaking," which here refers to the remembrance of Allah and the recitation of the Quran.

Altogether, this is one of the verses that, with its content-rich expressions, provides the most eloquent statement regarding "nocturnal worship," dawn supplication, and intimate discourse with the Beloved during hours when the means for peace of mind are most available. It also highlights its impact on the purification of the self and the nurturing of the human soul, showing that the human spirit has a special readiness for supplication, intimate prayer, remembrance, and reflection during those hours.

In the following verse, it adds: "This is because during the day you will have prolonged and continuous effort and occupation" (إِنَّ لَكَ فِي النَّهارِ سَبْحاً طَوِيلًا). You are constantly busy guiding the creation, conveying the message of your Lord, and solving collective and individual life problems, and sufficient opportunity for worship and supplication is not obtained; therefore, substitute it with nocturnal worship.

Another possibility in the interpretation of the verse, which is in some ways more interesting and more harmonious with the preceding and succeeding verses, is that it says: "Since you have heavy duties and great effort and struggle upon your shoulders during the day, you must strengthen yourself through nocturnal worship, and obtain the necessary readiness for these great and extensive activities from that nocturnal rising."

"Sabh" (سبح) originally means movement and coming and going, and is sometimes applied to swimming because it involves continuous movements. It is as if human society is likened to a boundless ocean in which a great multitude are drowning; its roaring waves move in every direction, wandering ships seek a haven, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is the only lifeguard in this sea, and His Quran is the only ship of salvation in this ocean. This great swimmer must prepare himself for this immense daily mission and message through nocturnal worship.

After the command for nocturnal worship and the brief reference to its deep effects, it proceeds to mention five complementary commands, stating: "And remember the name of your Lord" (وَ اذْكُرِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ). It is certain that the intention is not merely the verbal mention of the name, but rather attention to the meaning, for verbal remembrance is a prelude to the remembrance of the heart, and heart-centered remembrance brings clarity to the soul and waters the seedling of gnosis and piety in the heart. The use of the term "Rabb" (Lord) is an indication that whenever you mention His holy name, you should be mindful of His endless blessings and His continuous nurturing of you.

One of the commentators has described stages for the remembrance of the Lord: The first stage is the mention of His name, which is referred to in the verse under discussion. In the next stage, it is the turn of remembering His Pure Essence in the heart, which is mentioned in verse 205 of Surah Al-A'raf: "And remember your Lord within yourself in humility and in fear." Finally, the third stage arrives; in this stage, one transcends the station of Lordship (Rububiyyah) and reaches the station of the totality of the attributes of Beauty and Majesty of God, which are gathered in "Allah," as He says in verse 41 of Surah Al-Ahzab: "O you who have believed, remember Allah with much remembrance." In this way, this remembrance continues and evolves stage by stage, carrying its practitioner to the peak of perfection. ^1

In the second command, He says: "Devote yourself to Him and cut off hope from other than Him, and rise for His worship with sincerity" (وَ تَبَتَّلْ إِلَيْهِ تَبْتِيلًا) [^2]. "Tabattul" (تبتل) is derived from the root "batl" (بتل), which originally means "detachment" or "severance." Hence, Maryam is called "Batul" because she had not chosen any spouse for herself. And if the Lady of Islam, Fatima al-Zahra (سلام الله عليها), is also called "Batul," it is because she was distinct and superior to the women of her time in terms of deeds, behavior, and gnosis, and had reached the state of total devotion to Allah.

In any case, "Tabattul" is for a person to turn toward Allah with their whole heart, to be detached from everything other than Allah, to perform their deeds only for His sake, and to be immersed in sincerity. And if it is narrated in a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: “There is no monasticism and no tabattul in Islam,” ^1 it refers to the fact that the abandonment of the world in the sense common among Christian monks does not exist in Islam. They abandon both marriage and any kind of social activity to leave the world, but a true Muslim, while living in the heart of society, has their entire focus on Allah.

In some traditions from the Infallible Imams, it is narrated that "Tabattul" means raising the hands in prayer [^2], but it is clear that this is actually an expression of one of the manifestations of sincerity and detachment toward Allah. In any case, that remembrance of the Lord and this sincerity are the great capital of the men of God in the heavy program of guiding the creation.

^1: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 30, p. 177 (adapted). [^2]: According to the rule, the "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) here should have been "Tabattulan" (the gerund of the form Tafā'ul), but instead, the gerund of the form Taf'īl (Tabtīlan) is used. In addition to maintaining the harmony of the verse endings, this may point to a subtle point: that "detachment toward Allah" is neither entirely acquired nor entirely a gift; on one hand, the effort of the pious servant is a condition, and on the other hand, the grace of the Lord.

Then, addressing the third command, He adds: "The Lord of the East and the West, there is no deity except Him, so take Him as a Disposer of Affairs, Protector, and Helper" (رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَ الْمَغْرِبِ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَاتَّخِذْهُ وَكِيلًا). Here, after the stages of "remembrance of Allah" and "sincerity," the stage of trust (Tawakkul) and entrusting all affairs to Allah arrives—the God under whose sovereignty and Lordship the East and West of the world, meaning the entire universe, are placed, and He is the only deity worthy of worship. This expression is actually a rationale for the subject of trusting in Allah; how can a human not trust in Him and not entrust their work to Him, while in the expanse of the universe, there is no ruler, commander, bestower, nurturer, or deity other than Him?

Finally, in the fourth and fifth commands, He says: "And be patient over what they say, and avoid them with a gracious avoidance" (وَ اصْبِرْ عَلى‌ ما يَقُولُونَ وَ اهْجُرْهُمْ هَجْراً جَمِيلًا). Thus, here the station of "patience" and "avoidance" arrives, because in the path of calling toward the Truth, the slandering of enemies and their harassment are frequent. If a gardener wishes to pluck a rose, he must have patience and tolerance against the tongue of the thorn. Furthermore, sometimes indifference and distance are necessary here, both to remain safe from their evil and to give them a lesson through this method. However, this avoidance and distance must not mean the cessation of educational programs, preaching, and calling toward Allah.

In this way, the above verses provide a comprehensive and complete prescription for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and all those who walk in His path: to seek help from nocturnal worship and the supplication of the Lord at dawn, and then to water this seedling with the water of remembrance of Allah, sincerity, trust, patience, and gracious avoidance. What a comprehensive and beautiful prescription!

The expression "Lord of the East and the West" (رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَ الْمَغْرِبِ) refers to His sovereignty and Lordship over the entire universe, just as in everyday expressions we say: "So-and-so brought the East and West of the earth under his control," meaning the entire earth, not just the points of East and West.

"Hajran Jamilan" (gracious avoidance), as we mentioned, means avoidance coupled with compassion, invitation, and preaching toward the Truth, which is considered one of the educational methods at specific stages, and it never contradicts the issue of "Jihad" at other stages, as each has its place and every point its occasion. In other words, this distance is not indifference, but rather a type of attention itself. In any case, the view of some who consider the ruling of the above verse to be abrogated by the verses of Jihad is not correct.

The late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan regarding this verse: “This verse indicates the obligation of patience against harm for the one who calls to the religion, and interacting with the best of character and using gentleness so that they may be closer to responding.” ^1

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 379.

Ayahs

وَذَرْنِى وَٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُو۟لِى ٱلنَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلًا (١١)
Leave Me [to deal] with the deniers, the opulent, and give them a little respite. (11)
إِنَّ لَدَيْنَآ أَنكَالًا وَجَحِيمًا (١٢)
Indeed with Us are heavy fetters and a fierce fire, (12)
وَطَعَامًا ذَا غُصَّةٍ وَعَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٣)
and a food that chokes [those who eat it], and a painful punishment [prepared for] (13)
يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَٱلْجِبَالُ وَكَانَتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَثِيبًا مَّهِيلًا (١٤)
the day when the earth and the mountains will quake, and the mountains will be like dunes of shifting sand. (14)
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولًا شَـٰهِدًا عَلَيْكُمْ كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولًا (١٥)
Indeed We have sent to you an apostle, to be a witness to you, just as We sent an apostle to Pharaoh. (15)
فَعَصَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ أَخْذًا وَبِيلًا (١٦)
But Pharaoh disobeyed the apostle; so We seized him with a terrible seizing. (16)
فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِن كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْمًا يَجْعَلُ ٱلْوِلْدَٰنَ شِيبًا (١٧)
So, if you disbelieve, how will you avoid the day, which will make children white-headed, (17)
ٱلسَّمَآءُ مُنفَطِرٌۢ بِهِۦ ۚ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَفْعُولًا (١٨)
and wherein the sky will be rent apart? His promise is bound to be fulfilled. (18)
إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦ تَذْكِرَةٌ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا (١٩)
This is indeed a reminder. So let anyone who wishes take the way toward his Lord. (19)

Commentary

Leave the Account of These Arrogant Sinners to Me!

In the final verse of the preceding passage, there was an allusion to the obstructions, improper speech, and harassment caused by the enemies of Islam. The verses under discussion place them under a barrage of severe threats regarding worldly and otherworldly punishments from Allah, inviting them to reconsider their sinister plans. Simultaneously, these verses offer consolation and steadfastness to the early Muslims facing the harsh onslaught of these enemies.

First, He says: "Leave Me [to deal] with the deniers, the possessors of ease [and wealth], and give them respite for a little while." (وَ ذَرْنِي وَ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ وَ مَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلًا).

This means: their opponent is not you, but Me; leave their punishment and retribution to Me. Grant them a brief respite so that the argument may be fully established against them, their true nature may be revealed, and they may burden their backs with the weight of sin. Then, My punishment shall seize them by the throat! We know that only a short time passed before the Muslims grew strong and dealt heavy, crushing blows to the body of the enemy in the battles of "Badr," "Hunayn," "Ahzab," and the like. Furthermore, it was not long before these tyrants departed from this world and were overtaken by divine punishment in the Barzakh (purgatory), and the punishment of Resurrection is not far from them either.

Additionally, the expression "أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ" (possessors of ease/blessings) refers to the arrogance and negligence resulting from an abundance of wealth and material means, which often afflicts their owners. For this reason, throughout the history of the Prophets—as the Quran bears witness—this group has always stood in the front rank of the opposition.

In truth, this verse is similar to verse 34 of Surah Saba, which states: "And We did not send a warner to any city except that its affluent ones said, 'Indeed we are disbelievers in that with which you were sent.'" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ إِلَّا قالَ مُتْرَفُوها إِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ).

This is while such individuals should have been the first to respond to the call of the heralds of Truth, so they might express gratitude for all these divine blessings through this path.

Then, continuing this threat in a more explicit manner, He says: "Indeed, with Us are shackles and a burning fire." (إِنَّ لَدَيْنا أَنْكالًا وَ جَحِيماً).

"Ankal" is the plural of "Nikl" (rhyming with fikr), meaning heavy chains. It is originally derived from the root "Nukul," meaning weakness and inability. Since the chains placed on the hands, feet, and neck prevent a person from moving and render them helpless, this word is used to mean "chains." Yes, in exchange for the unconditional freedom and luxury they enjoyed in this world, their portion there is captivity and fire!

And He further adds: "And food that chokes and a painful punishment." (وَ طَعاماً ذا غُصَّةٍ وَ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

This is food that is the opposite of the fatty and sweet foods of their world, which went down the throat easily and was pleasant. It is a painful life in contrast to the boundless comfort of these self-centered and arrogant people in this world.

Despite the fact that coarse and choking food is itself a painful punishment, He mentions "painful punishment" separately afterward. This indicates that the dimensions of the painful punishment of the Hereafter, in terms of intensity and magnitude, are known to no one but Allah. Therefore, we read in a narration that one day one of the Muslims was reciting this verse while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was listening; suddenly, the person let out a cry and fainted [1]. In another narration, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself was reciting this verse and fell into such a state [2]. How could this not be choking food? While in verse 6 of Surah Al-Ghashiyah we read: "They will have no food except from a poisonous, thorny plant." (لَيْسَ لَهُمْ طَعامٌ إِلَّا مِنْ ضَرِيعٍ).

And also in verses 43 and 44 of Surah Ad-Dukhan, we read: "Indeed, the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful." (إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ).

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 380. [2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 107.

"The tree of Zaqqum (that bitter, foul-smelling, foul-tasting, and lethal plant) is the food of the sinners!"

Then He proceeds to describe the day when these punishments will manifest, saying: "On the Day the earth and the mountains convulse and the mountains become a heap of sand pouring down." (يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ وَ كانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلًا).

"Kathib" means accumulated sand, and "Mahil" is from the root hayl (rhyming with kayl), meaning to pour a soft substance like sand or flour over something. Here, it refers to soft sand that has no stability or firmness.

According to this meaning, the mountains on the Day of Resurrection will be so disintegrated that they become like soft sand; if one were to step on it, they would sink.

Regarding the fate of the mountains on the threshold of Resurrection, the Quran uses various expressions, all of which indicate their destruction and transformation into soft dust (a more detailed explanation regarding the different stages of the destruction of mountains and the varying Quranic expressions in this regard is provided under verse 105 of Surah Ta-Ha [1]). Then, He makes a comparison between the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the opposition of the powerful Arabs, and the uprising of Musa bin 'Imran عليه السلام against the Pharaohs, saying: "Indeed, We have sent to you a Messenger as a witness over you, just as We sent to Pharaoh a messenger." (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْكُمْ رَسُولًا شاهِداً عَلَيْكُمْ كَما أَرْسَلْنا إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ رَسُولًا).

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 13, p. 302.

His goal is your guidance and the supervision of your deeds, just as the goal of Musa bin 'Imran عليه السلام was the guidance of Pharaoh and his people and the supervision of their deeds.

"But Pharaoh disobeyed the messenger, so We seized him with a ruinous seizure." (فَعَصى‌ فِرْعَوْنُ الرَّسُولَ فَأَخَذْناهُ أَخْذاً وَبِيلًا).

Neither his massive army prevented the divine punishment, nor did the vastness of his kingdom, the power of his government, or his wealth and affluent supporters stop it. Ultimately, they all drowned in the surging waves of the Nile, in which they used to take pride. You, who are at a much lower level than them and are far weaker than Pharaoh and his people in terms of numbers and equipment—what do you think of yourselves?! And how can you be deceived by your meager wealth and followers?! "Wabil" is from the root wabl, which originally means heavy and intense rain, and subsequently, it is applied to anything intense and heavy, especially regarding punishment. In the verse under discussion, it refers to the intensity of the punishment, as if it places individuals under a barrage like heavy rain.

Then, addressing the disbelievers of the time of the Prophet of Islam, He warns them: "Then how can you guard yourselves, if you disbelieve, against a Day that will make the children white-haired?" (فَكَيْفَ تَتَّقُونَ إِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ يَوْماً يَجْعَلُ الْوِلْدانَ شِيباً) [1] & [2].

[1]: "Yawman" (a Day) is the object of "tattaqun," and guarding against that day means guarding against the punishment of that day. Some have suggested it is an adverb for "tattaqun" or the object of "kafartum," both of which seem unlikely. [2]: "Shib" (rhyming with sib) is the plural of "ashyab," meaning "old/white-haired." It is originally from the root shiyb (rhyming with ayb) and mashib, meaning the whitening of the hair.

Yes, the punishment of that day is so great, heavy, intense, terrifying, and back-breaking that it turns children old. This is a metaphor for its intensity. Let alone the punishment of the Hereafter, some report that even in this world, when a person is placed under extraordinary pressure, it is perceptible that in a fleeting moment, their hair turns white! In any case, the above verse indicates that even if you are not overtaken by a destructive punishment in this world like the Pharaohs, what will you do about the Resurrection and its punishment?!

In the next verse, He provides more description of that terrifying day, adding: "The heaven will break apart therefrom; His promise is ever to be fulfilled." (السَّماءُ مُنْفَطِرٌ بِهِ كانَ وَعْدُهُ مَفْعُولًا).

Many verses regarding the Resurrection and the "Signs of the Hour" (events occurring at the end of the world and the beginning of the Resurrection) speak of massive explosions, severe earthquakes, and rapid transformations, of which the verse under discussion mentions only a part.

When the heavens and the celestial bodies, with all their greatness, cannot withstand the massive events of that day, what can this weak, helpless, and vulnerable human do?! [1]

[1]: "Munfatir" is from the root "infitar," meaning splitting and breaking apart. The pronoun in "bihi" refers to "yawm" (the Day), meaning the heaven is split apart by means of that day (note that "sama'" is used as both masculine and feminine).

At the conclusion of this discussion, He refers to all the previous warnings and admonitions, saying: "Indeed, this is a reminder." (إِنَّ هذِهِ تَذْكِرَةٌ).

You are free in choosing your path, "so whoever wills, let him take a way to his Lord." (فَمَنْ شاءَ اتَّخَذَ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا).

If traversing this path were through compulsion and coercion, it would be neither an honor nor a virtue. Virtue lies in a person choosing and walking this path by their own will and choice.

In short, Allah has shown the way and the pit, provided the seeing eye and the illuminating sun, and given people the choice to act with will and determination in obeying His command.

Regarding what the phrase "Indeed, this is a reminder" refers to, several possibilities have been suggested by commentators: sometimes it is said to refer to the exhortations in the preceding verses, sometimes to the entire Surah, or to the entire Quran.

Some have also said it might refer to the command for prayer and nocturnal worship mentioned in the first verses of the Surah, where the addressee was the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; this phrase intends to generalize and extend it to other Muslims. Therefore, the "way" (sabil) in the following phrase would also be the Night Prayer, which is among the important ways to the Lord, just as in verse 26 of Surah Ad-Dahr, after the command for nocturnal worship ("And during the night prostrate to Him and exalt Him a long [part of the] night"), it says after a short interval: "Indeed, this is a reminder, so whoever wills, let him take a way to his Lord," which is exactly the same as the verse under discussion [1].

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 147.

Of course, this is a suitable interpretation, but it is more appropriate for the concept of the verse to be broad, encompassing all the soul-building programs of this Surah, as we mentioned above.

Key Point: The Four Stages of Divine Punishment

In the above verses, the arrogant deniers who are intoxicated with blessings are threatened with four painful punishments: shackles (ankal), the blazing fire of Hell (jahim), coarse, choking, and lethal food (wa ta'aman dha ghussah), and other types of painful punishments that do not cross the human mind (wa 'adhaban alima). These punishments are, in fact, the exact opposite of their condition in this worldly life. On one hand, they had limitless freedom, on the other, a luxurious life, thirdly, pleasant food, and fourthly, various comforts. Since they acquired all of these at the cost of oppression, violating the rights of others, pride, arrogance, and negligence of God, they will face such a fate on the Day of Resurrection.

Ayah

۞ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ثُلُثَىِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَنِصْفَهُۥ وَثُلُثَهُۥ وَطَآئِفَةٌ مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مَعَكَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُقَدِّرُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ عَلِمَ أَن لَّن تُحْصُوهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ عَلِمَ أَن سَيَكُونُ مِنكُم مَّرْضَىٰ ۙ وَءَاخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ ۙ وَءَاخَرُونَ يُقَـٰتِلُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَٱقْرَءُوا۟ مَا تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ ۚ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا ۚ وَمَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ لِأَنفُسِكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْرًا وَأَعْظَمَ أَجْرًا ۚ وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌۢ (٢٠)
Indeed your Lord knows that you stand vigil for nearly two thirds of the night—or [at times] a half or a third of it—along with a group of those who are with you. Allah measures the night and the day. He knows that you cannot calculate it [exactly], and so He was lenient toward you. So recite as much of the Quran as is feasible. He knows that some of you will be sick, while others will travel in the land seeking Allah’s bounty, and yet others will fight in the way of Allah. So recite as much of it as is feasible, and maintain the prayer and pay the zakat and lend Allah a good loan. Whatever good you send ahead for your souls you will find it with Allah [in a form] that is better and greater with respect to reward. And plead to Allah for forgiveness; indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (20)

Commentary

Recite as much of the Quran as is possible for you

This verse, which is the longest verse of this Surah, contains many matters that complete the content of the preceding verses.

There is intense discussion among commentators as to whether this verse abrogates the instructions of the verses at the beginning of this Surah, or serves as an explanation and commentary for them, and also whether this verse was revealed in Makkah or Madinah.

The answer to these questions will become clear after the commentary on the verse.

First, He says: "Indeed, your Lord knows that you and a group of those with you stand [in prayer] nearly two-thirds of the night, or half of it, or a third of it. How could Allah not be aware of it, while the measuring of the night and the day is by Him?" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّكَ تَقُومُ أَدْنى‌ مِنْ ثُلُثَيِ اللَّيْلِ وَ نِصْفَهُ وَ ثُلُثَهُ وَ طائِفَةٌ مِنَ الَّذِينَ مَعَكَ وَ اللَّهُ يُقَدِّرُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ) [^1] This refers to the same instruction given to the Prophet at the beginning of the Surah.

[^1]: It should be noted that "نصفه" (half of it) and "ثلثه" (a third of it) are conjoined to "ادنى" (nearly/less than), not to "ثلثى الليل" (two-thirds of the night). Therefore, the meaning of the sentence becomes: about two-thirds of the night, or exactly half, or one-third of the night. Additionally, it should be noted that "ادنى" is usually used for physical proximity (nearness in space), but here it refers to approximate time.

The only addition here is that a group of believers also accompanied the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this nightly worship (either as a recommended act or possibly an obligatory one, because the conditions of early Islam required them to build themselves through the recitation of the Quran—which contained various ideological, practical, and moral lessons—and nightly worship, preparing them to propagate and defend Islam).

However, as inferred from some narrations, a group of Muslims faced difficulty and trouble in keeping track of the "third," "half," and "two-thirds" (since means of measuring time did not exist in that era). Consequently, they were forced to exercise caution, which sometimes led them to stay awake the entire night engaged in worship, to the point that their feet swelled from the nightly standing! Therefore, Allah eased this ruling for them and said: "He knows that you cannot accurately count [the time], so He has turned to you in mercy; now recite as much of the Quran as is easy for you" (عَلِمَ أَنْ لَنْ تُحْصُوهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ‌).

"لن تحصوه" is from the root "احصاء" meaning to count or enumerate; meaning you cannot precisely determine the time of night in terms of two-thirds, half, or one-third, and you fall into hardship.

Some have also said it means you cannot maintain this practice consistently throughout all days of the year.

Even today, when there are means to wake up on time, precisely determining these portions throughout the year—especially with the constant variation in the length of night and day—is not an easy task.

Most commentators have interpreted the phrase "فَتابَ عَلَيْكُمْ‌" (He turned to you) as "lightening this duty," not in the sense of "repentance from sin." It is also possible that when the ruling of obligation is lifted, no sin occurs, and consequently, it is like divine forgiveness.

Regarding what is meant by the phrase "فاقرؤواما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ‌" (recite what is easy for you of the Quran), there is much discussion: a group has interpreted it as the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl), during which Quranic verses are certainly recited. Others have said it refers to the recitation of the Quran itself, even if not during prayer. Then, some have interpreted its amount as fifty verses, some as one hundred, and some as two hundred; however, none of these numbers have specific evidence. Rather, the meaning of the verse is that one should recite as much of the Quran as does not cause them hardship.

It is obvious that "recitation of the Quran" here refers to recitation as a lesson and learning for self-building and the cultivation of faith and piety.

Then, stating another reason for this lightening of the burden, He adds: "Allah knows that a group among you will be ill, and others will be traveling through the land seeking the bounty of Allah [for livelihood], and others will be fighting in the way of Allah; and these matters will prevent them from continuously performing the nightly worship in the measure previously determined" (عَلِمَ أَنْ سَيَكُونُ مِنْكُمْ مَرْضى‌ وَ آخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَبْتَغُونَ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَ آخَرُونَ يُقاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌).

And this is another reason for the easing of this program; hence He repeats once more: "Since it is so, recite from the Quran as much as is possible and within your ability during the night" (فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنْهُ‌).

It is clear that the mention of illness, necessary travels, and Jihad in the way of Allah are three examples of valid excuses, but they are not limited to these. The point is that since Allah knows you will be occupied with various life problems during the day, and this prevents the continuation of that heavy program, He has granted you a concession.

Now the question arises: does this ruling abrogate what came at the beginning of the Surah? Or is it an exception to it? The appearance of the verses suggests the abrogation of the previous ruling. In reality, it was necessary for this program to be implemented for a period, and it was; the purpose of this ruling, which had a temporary and extraordinary nature, was achieved. After this period ended, it remained in a lighter form, because the appearance of the verse is that due to the existence of those with excuses, this ruling was lightened for everyone, not just for the group with excuses. Thus, it cannot be an exception but must be an abrogation (reflect on this). Here another question arises: is reciting the possible amount of the Quran, which is commanded twice in this verse, obligatory or recommended?

Some have said: it is certainly recommended. Others have considered the possibility of it being obligatory, because reciting the Quran leads to awareness of the proofs of Monotheism, the sending of messengers, the miracle of this Heavenly Book, and the learning of other religious obligations. Therefore, reciting the Quran is a prerequisite for an obligation and is thus obligatory.

However, it should be noted that in this case, it is not necessary to recite it at night or during the Night Prayer; rather, it is obligatory for every accountable person (mukallaf) to recite the amount necessary for education, training, and awareness of the principles and branches of Islam, as well as for preserving the Quran and conveying it to future generations, without a specific time being required for it.

But the truth is that the appearance of the command in the phrase "فاقرءوا ..." (recite...) is one of obligation (as explained in the Principles of Jurisprudence), unless it is said that this "command," by the evidence of the "consensus of jurists on its non-obligation," is a recommended command. The result is that at the beginning of Islam, due to certain conditions, this recitation and nightly worship were obligatory, and later it was lightened both in terms of quantity and ruling, becoming a recommended act to the extent of one's ease. However, in any case, the obligation of the Night Prayer remained fixed for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله until the end of his life (by the evidence of other Quranic verses and narrations).

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we also read: The ruling regarding two-thirds of the night, or half of it, or a third of it, has been abrogated, and in its place "فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ‌" (recite what is easy of the Quran) has been established. [^1] It is noteworthy that three types of excuses are mentioned in the verse under discussion: one is physical (illness), another is financial (traveling for business), and the third is religious (Jihad in the way of Allah). Therefore, some have said it can be inferred from this verse that striving for livelihood is on par with Jihad in the way of Allah! They have also taken this phrase as evidence that this specific verse was revealed in Madinah, because the obligation of Jihad did not exist in Makkah. However, considering that He says "سيكون" (it will soon be), this phrase might be news of the legislation of Jihad in the future; meaning, because you have excuses now and other excuses will arise for you in the future, this ruling did not become permanent. In this way, it does not contradict the verse being Makkan.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 451.

Then, He points to four other instructions at the end of this verse, completing the presented self-building program: "Establish prayer, and give zakat, and give Allah a goodly loan through recommended spending, and know that whatever good you send forth for yourselves, you will find it with Allah as better and greater in reward." (وَ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ آتُوا الزَّكاةَ وَ أَقْرِضُوا اللَّهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً وَ ما تُقَدِّمُوا لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ تَجِدُوهُ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ هُوَ خَيْراً وَ أَعْظَمَ أَجْراً).

"And seek forgiveness from Allah, for Allah is Forgiving and Merciful" (وَ اسْتَغْفِرُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

These four instructions (prayer, zakat, recommended spending, and seeking forgiveness), combined with the instruction to recite and contemplate the Quran mentioned in the previous sentences, together form a complete self-building program that has had and continues to have an undeniable impact in every age, especially at the dawn of Islam.

The meaning of "prayer" here is the five obligatory prayers, and "zakat" refers to the obligatory zakat. Giving a "goodly loan" to Allah refers to recommended spending (infaq), and this is the most generous expression imaginable in this regard; for the Owner of all kingdoms asks for a loan from one who absolutely owns nothing of his own, in order to encourage him toward spending, altruism, and gaining the virtue of this good deed, so that he may be disciplined and reach perfection through this path.

The mention of seeking forgiveness at the end of these instructions may be an indication that one should not, by performing these acts of obedience, consider themselves a perfect human being or, so to speak, a "creditor." Rather, one must always consider themselves falling short and bring excuses to the threshold of God, for "no one can fulfill the service worthy of His Divinity."

Some commentators believe the emphasis on these instructions is to prevent the thought that if a concession was granted regarding the nightly standing and recitation of the Quran, it extends to other religious programs and instructions; rather, they remain in full force. [^1] Additionally, the mention of obligatory zakat here has been taken as another proof of this verse being Madinan, as the ruling of zakat was revealed in Madinah, not Makkah. However, some have said the essence of zakat was revealed in Makkah, but no threshold (nisab) or amount was specified for it; what was legislated in Madinah was the issue of the threshold and the specific amount of zakat.

Key Points

1- The Necessity of Ideological and Cultural Preparedness

To perform important social tasks, especially creating a vast and extensive revolution in all aspects of life, a determined human force is required above all else—one built for this task with firm belief, complete awareness, necessary intellectual and cultural training, and moral cultivation.

And this was exactly the work that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله performed in Makkah during the early years of his mission, and indeed throughout his life. For this reason, and because of this solid foundation, the seedling of Islam grew rapidly, budded, and brought forth branches and leaves.

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Mizan," Vol. 20, p. 156.

What appears in this Surah is a living and very eloquent example of this calculated program: the command to worship for two-thirds or at least one-third of the night, coupled with the recitation and careful study of the verses of the Holy Quran, left a strange impact on the souls of the Muslims and prepared them to accept the "heavy word" and "long occupation." These nightly movements and risings, which in the words of the Quran were "more effective for coordination" and "more upright in speech," eventually did their work. A small group from the deprived masses and the oppressed classes were so built up that they gained the merit to rule over a vast portion of the world.

And today, if we Muslims wish to regain our ancient greatness and power, the path is this very path, and the program is this very program. We must never expect to overthrow the "Zionist" hegemony from the heart of Islamic countries or cut off the hands of the bullies and super-criminals from Islamic lands with individuals who are weak in thought and faith, and who have not received the necessary cultural and moral cultivation. This is an extensive discussion, but "for the one who understands, a single word is enough!"

2- Reciting the Quran with Contemplation

It is well-understood from Islamic narrations that the virtue of reciting the Quran does not lie in reading a great quantity of it, but in reading it well and with contemplation and reflection.

Interestingly, regarding the above verse which commands "recite what is easy of it," a narration is recorded from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, who quotes his grandfather as saying: "Recite that amount in which there is humility of the heart, purity of the inner self, and spiritual and celestial joy!" [^1] Why should it not be so, when the primary goal of recitation is education and training?

And narrations in this regard are numerous.

3- Striving for Livelihood is on Par with Jihad

In the above verse—as we saw—striving for life is placed alongside "Jihad in the way of Allah," and this shows that Islam places great importance on this subject. Why should it not be so, when a poor, hungry nation dependent on foreigners will never achieve independence, greatness, and pride? Fundamentally, "economic Jihad" is a part of "Jihad against the enemy."

In this regard, a statement is narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, the famous companion, who says: "Any man who brings goods to one of the cities of the Muslims, being patient and seeking reward from Allah, then sells it at the fair price of that day, he is in the sight of Allah at the rank of the martyrs." Then he recited "وَ آخَرُونَ يَضْرِبُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌" (and others traveling through the land...) as evidence. [^2]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 382. [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," "Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi," and "Tafsir Qurtubi" under the verse discussed. Additionally, from another similar Hadith narrated in Tafsir Qurtubi from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is understood that Abdullah ibn Mas'ud did not say this of his own accord but learned it from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

O Allah! Grant us the success of Jihad in all its dimensions. O Lord! Bestow upon us all the success of the nightly standing, the recitation of the Holy Quran, and building ourselves in the light of this Heavenly illumination. O Lord! Lead our Islamic society, inspired by this content-rich Surah, to its worthy status and ancient greatness.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Muzzammil. 1st of Safar, 1407 (October 14, 1986).
